title: Pointer Networks
authors: Oriol Vinyals, Meire Fortunato, Navdeep Jaitly
hook: Pointer networks let neural models select positions as outputs, not just fixed tokens.
promise: You will understand how pointer networks enable neural nets to solve problems with variable output sizes.
prerequisite_note: You only need a sense for how sequence models usually output from a fixed set of tokens.

glossary.plain: A neural network that outputs positions from the input sequence, not predefined tokens.
A method that lets the model focus on specific parts of the input when deciding outputs.
A situation where the number of possible outputs changes depending on the input size.
A neural model that turns one sequence into another, usually outputting from a fixed vocabulary.
Finding the best solution from a large set of discrete possibilities, like the shortest route in a graph.
The smallest convex shape that contains all points in a set.

cards.title: Pick Positions, Not Tokens
body: See how pointer networks let neural models output positions from the input, not just fixed vocabulary tokens.

cards.title: Why fixed vocabularies limit neural models
body: Imagine you want a neural network to sort a list, or pick the shortest route through cities. Each answer is a list of positions, not words.
Traditional sequence models can only pick from a set vocabulary. But if your input changes size, you need a way to point directly to any input element.

cards.title: From tokens to pointers
body: First, the model reads the input sequence, encoding each element into a vector. Then, at every output step, it uses attention to select one input position.
Instead of picking a word from a dictionary, the model points to a location in the input. This lets it handle tasks where output size matches input size, like sorting.
cards.middle: Attend and point to an input position
cards.right: Output is a sequence of input positions
cards.caption: Pointer networks use attention as a pointer, not just a way to blend information.

cards.title: How do outputs change?
body: Suppose you want to sort a list of numbers of different lengths.
cards.offNote: Model can only output from a set list of tokens, so variable output is hard.
cards.onNote: Model can select any input position at each step, so output adapts to input size.

cards.title: Turning attention into a pointer
body: Pointer networks use attention differently. Instead of blending input information, attention acts as a pointer, letting the model select a specific input element as output.
This is crucial for problems where the output must reference parts of the input directly, like finding a convex hull or solving the traveling salesman problem.

cards.title: What happens as input size grows?
body: Adjust the input length and see how output flexibility and complexity change.
cards.low: Few input positions, output is simple and fixed.
cards.mid: More positions, model must keep track of more possibilities.
cards.high: Many positions, model can point to any but must manage complex combinatorial choices.

cards.title: What makes a pointer network?
body: Choose the key components needed to build a pointer network.

choices.label: Attention mechanism
choices.note: Lets the model focus on specific input positions.

choices.label: Recurrent decoder
choices.note: Produces output sequence step by step.

choices.label: Training with examples
choices.note: Learns to point by seeing input-output pairs.

cards.title: Pointer networks solve hard geometric problems
body: Pointer networks were tested on computing convex hulls, Delaunay triangulations, and traveling salesman tours. They could generalize to longer sequences than they saw during training.
Compared to standard sequence-to-sequence models, pointer networks achieved much higher accuracy on tasks where outputs must reference input positions directly.
cards.caption: Pointer networks enable neural models to tackle combinatorial problems with variable-sized outputs.

cards.title: A new way to output sequences
body: Pointer networks broke a barrier by letting neural models point to any input position, not just emit preset tokens.
This innovation opened neural networks to a new class of problems, like sorting and routing, where outputs must directly reference parts of the input.

recap.label: Encode
recap.note: Turn input elements into vector representations.

recap.label: Attend
recap.note: Focus on one input position at each step.

recap.label: Point
recap.note: Select that position as the output for the step.

recap.label: Repeat
recap.note: Continue until the output sequence is complete.
